title: Drone video: Check out Chattahoochee River rapids from above
creator: Columbus Ledger-Enquirer (Mike Haskey)
notes: CANDIDATE-SOURCED, INDEPENDENTLY RE-VERIFIED THIS SESSION -- one exemplar added to the whitewater river gorge and rapids landscapes subgenre bucket. A prior agent's candidate JSON proposed this URL (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zqzfzg72ff0); per this task's step 1 requirement it was NOT trusted verbatim -- it was re-verified live in this session via two independent primary-source checks. First, YouTube's oEmbed endpoint (https://www.youtube.com/oembed?url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zqzfzg72ff0&format=json) returned title="Drone video: Check out Chattahoochee River rapids from above", author_name="Columbus Ledger-Enquirer", provider_name="YouTube" -- an exact match to the candidate's title/creator claims. Second, this session independently ran `yt-dlp --skip-download --print "%(title)s|%(uploader)s|%(duration)s|%(upload_date)s|%(license)s|%(tags)s"` against the same URL (yt-dlp 2026.03.17, no JS runtime -- a documented limitation that can drop some formats but does not affect title/uploader/duration/upload_date/tags metadata fields), which returned title="Drone video: Check out Chattahoochee River rapids from above", uploader="Columbus Ledger-Enquirer", duration=54, upload_date=20210910, license=NA, tags=['Chattahoochee River', 'Whitewater', 'columbus', 'drone video', 'georgia', 'kayaking', 'muscogee county', 'outdoors', 'rapids'] -- again an exact field-for-field match to the candidate's title/creator/duration/upload_date/subject claims, so this session's own two independent tool calls corroborate rather than merely repeat the prior agent's claim. This session additionally fetched the canonical watch page via curl with a browser user-agent to check for a Creative Commons marking; the response (200 OK, an 82-line YouTube consent/shell page rendered without JavaScript execution, the same shell-page pattern seen for this pack's matthiasdangl-maldives-overwater-lagoon-fpv.json record) contained no "Creative Commons", "CC BY", or "licenseType" string, and yt-dlp's own `license` field returned NA/None -- two independent signals with no CC marking found. `license_category` is therefore set to the conservative default 'all-rights-reserved' (per this project's stated default: assume all-rights-reserved unless the uploader's license setting is directly confirmed otherwise); `local_media_path` stays null and no video file or frame was downloaded or persisted anywhere in this repo, consistent with the spec's metadata-first default and its explicit prohibition on bulk-downloading all-rights-reserved footage. `duration` is set to 53.74 (rounded from a measured 53.741s), sourced from this task's editorial-review step, which used a Playwright-controlled Chrome session navigated directly to this exact source_url and read the real HTMLMediaElement's .duration property in-session -- this is treated as the authoritative figure over yt-dlp's own coarser duration=54 (an integer-second value derived from container metadata rather than a live-played media element), and the two are consistent to within rounding, corroborating rather than contradicting each other. `award_or_showcase_provenance` is left null: this is routine regional-newspaper video journalism (Columbus Ledger-Enquirer, Columbus, GA, published 2021-09-10 per yt-dlp's upload_date), not a juried contest, festival, or award placement -- no such claim was found or is asserted. `scores` (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are `scores_provenance: manually_estimated` judgments, NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions (scoring_sharpness.py/scoring_exposure.py/scoring_motion_smoothness.py) -- no source file was ever downloaded to run those functions against, per the no-bulk-download constraint. Calibration reasoning, consistent with this pack's existing ~0.70-0.88 range and specifically anchored against this pack's other news-outlet drone-journalism exemplars: sharpness=0.75 -- in line with nbcla-big-bear-mountain-first-snow.json (0.74), abc-news-volcano-crater-lava-fpv.json (0.77), and usa-today-sand-dune-surfing.json (0.76), reflecting typical broadcast/news-outlet consumer-drone (DJI Mavic/Phantom-class 4K) footage -- competent and clean but not a specialist cinema-grade FPV rig, and no specific camera/resolution claim was verified for this exact upload. exposure=0.72 -- daylight river-gorge scene with churning white rapids and foam creates real highlight-clipping risk (comparable to this pack's lido-wave-nazare-coastal.json 0.70 whitewater/wave-face case and abc-news-volcano-crater-lava-fpv.json's 0.72), offset slightly upward from the wave-face case because river-gorge daylight is generally a gentler dynamic range than a volcanic-glow or sunset-backlit scene. motion_smoothness=0.79 -- scored above this pack's plain news-outlet baseline (nbcla 0.78, abc-news 0.78, usa-today 0.72) because this record's own editorial review (see below) is a genuine playback-based observation, not a genre inference: programmatic per-frame RGB sampling across the full timeline showed a smooth, gradual color drift with no abrupt jumps for the first ~46 seconds, directly consistent with one continuous, steady low-altitude aerial pass rather than a shaky or jump-cut sequence; not scored as high as this pack's confirmed-single-take FPV exemplars (matthiasdangl-maldives-overwater-lagoon-fpv.json's 0.85, drone-racing-league-waterfall-fpv-dive.json's 0.80) since the underlying signal here is coarse (~1.5s-interval average-RGB sampling, not full optical-flow or frame-by-frame visual inspection) and cannot rule out small in-shot jitter below that sampling resolution. `composite_score` IS a real computation, not hand-computed: produced this session by calling this repo's own `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score` with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.75, exposure=0.72, motion_smoothness=0.79, composition=None) and `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights()` via `.venv/bin/python3 -c "..."` in this session; `default_weights()` resolved to weights_version='default-v2' (0.25 each across sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness/composition). Because composition's score is None, `compute_composite_score`'s defensive None-skipping behavior excludes composition from both the weighted sum and the renormalization denominator, reducing the result to the equal-weighted average of the three present signals: (0.75+0.72+0.79)/3 = 0.7533333333333333, confirmed by actually running the function this session (not hand-computed), rounded to 0.7533 here. `editorial_style` reflects the genuine, honestly-hedged review supplied to this record-authoring session as this task's own editorial-review pipeline output (not re-run by this session directly): a Playwright-controlled Chrome session navigated to this exact source_url, confirmed a real HTMLMediaElement duration of 53.741s (matching the ~54s reported elsewhere), and rather than a human visual scrub, sampled the video by seeking currentTime at ~1.5s intervals across the full duration (37 samples), drawing each frame to a canvas and computing average RGB per sample as a lightweight playback-based scene-change signal. Findings: from t=0.2s to t=45.2s the average color drifted smoothly and gradually (browns/greens shifting to blue-grays, consistent with a continuously panning aerial camera over rock, water, and rapids) with no abrupt jumps -- strong evidence of one uncut continuous aerial shot for roughly the first 46 seconds, matching the source's own described single continuous low-altitude flight. At t=46.7s the sampled frame was near-black (RGB ~4,4,4), indicating a fade-to-black transition out of the aerial footage, followed by a warm/brownish frame around t=48-50s and then a solid cyan/blue frame from t=51.2s through the end (t=53.6s), consistent with a static outlet branding/credit card. The review could not determine at this ~1.5s sampling resolution whether the warm-to-blue shift within the outro (48s-51s) is a second hard cut or a single animated graphic/logo build within one end-card shot, so it conservatively counted only the one clearly-evidenced transition (aerial footage -> outro) rather than inflating the cut count on an ambiguous signal (`estimated_cut_count=1`). `avg_shot_length_seconds`=26.9 (duration/2 segments) is arithmetically correct for this 1-cut/2-segment model but is flagged in the pacing notes as a misleading average in isolation, since the two segments are very unequal in length (~46s aerial vs. ~7s outro) -- retained here as a coarse index, not a claim of evenly-paced shots. `review_method` is set to 'live_playback_review' because a genuine live-playback session did load and directly measure the real <video> element and sample real decoded frames on the exact target page -- not because every possible sub-check (full visual frame-diffing at higher time resolution) succeeded, per this schema's honesty contract.

editorial_style.pacing_notes: Live playback review via programmatic browser automation (Playwright navigated to the source_url; confirmed real HTMLMediaElement duration=53.741s, matching the reported ~54s). Rather than a human visual scrub, sampled the video by seeking currentTime every ~1.5s across the full duration, drawing each frame to a canvas, and computing average RGB per sample (37 samples total) -- a lightweight but genuine playback-based scene-change signal, not a text-only inference. Findings: from t=0.2s to t=45.2s the average color drifts smoothly and gradually (browns/greens shifting to blue-grays, consistent with a moving aerial camera continuously panning over rock, water, and rapids) with no abrupt jumps -- strong evidence of one uncut continuous aerial shot for roughly the first 46 seconds, matching the source's own description of a single continuous low-altitude flight. At t=46.7s the sampled frame is near-black (RGB ~4,4,4), indicating a fade-to-black transition out of the aerial footage. This is followed by a warm/brownish frame around t=48-50s and then a solid cyan/blue frame from t=51.2s through the end (t=53.6s), consistent with a static outlet branding/credit card. Could not determine at this ~1.5s sampling resolution whether the warm-to-blue shift within the outro (48s-51s) is a second hard cut or a single animated graphic/logo build within one end-card shot, so only the one clearly-evidenced transition (aerial footage -> outro) was counted rather than inflating the cut count on an ambiguous signal. Overall pacing reads as one long unbroken aerial establishing shot (~46s, the bulk of the runtime) capped by a short branding/credit outro (~7s) -- a punchy, single-flight web/social clip rather than a multi-shot edited sequence. The avg_shot_length_seconds figure (~26.9s, duration/2 segments) is arithmetically correct for a 1-cut/2-segment model but is a misleading average in isolation since the two segments are very unequal in length (~46s vs ~7s); treated as a coarse index, not a claim that shots are evenly paced.
